sentences.text: Yes.
So this is it.
We're almost ready.
Is it-- everything OK?
Good, good.
You want me to speak in a different language now?
[LAUGHTER]
French?
[SPEAKING FRENCH] [LAUGHTER]
Und Deutsch, yeah, and-- and-- and [NON-ENGLISH].
[LAUGHTER]
No.
No Hebrew.
Yes.
My name is Eva, E-V-A, Shane, S-H-A-N-E.
My name at birth was Eva Cohn.
Ava, actually, in German-- Cohn, C-O-H-N.
My parents and close friends and relatives called me Moahrchen, which is a German word for little Moor, because I was-- I had a lot of dark hair, and they said she looks like a little Moor.
So they kept naming me there for many, many years.
Some-- a couple of people still do occasionally.
Moahrchen, yes.
That's M-O- [SIGHS] A-H-R-C-H-E-N.
My birth date was April 21st, 1928.
My age today, I just turned 67.
Hamburg, Altona, Germany.
Altona being the old part of Hamburg.
And at one time, it was a separate city, belonging to Denmark.
Later, it was melted in with Hamburg in one city, and became German.
I think in-- I don't know exactly what's year, but it's quite while ago.
Yes.
My father was a well-known criminal lawyer in Hamburg, Dr.
Carl Cohn.
[INAUDIBLE] they had this title Germans use-- love to use the title of Doctor.
And so he had-- I guess it means PhD or something.
And he had a rather large office downtown in Hamburg.
My mother worked for him as a secretary, one of his secretaries.
And she had worked there for quite a while when, well, they kind of fell in love and they ended up getting married.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was from a Jewish Orthodox background.
My mother was not Jewish, Gentile.
So there was kind of a problem.
And it took them seven years before they could get married, especially since my father was a Cohn and he only wanted an Orthodox rabbi to marry them.
So they-- he traveled all over trying to find a rabbi who would be able to do that until he found one in Budapest, in Hungary, where he was during a business trip.
And this Orthodox rabbi studied the Talmud and he found-- I don't remember what it was, but he said it's OK.
He found a loophole.
And so my father sent a telegram to my mother, and he says, come right away and let's get married here.
It's now or never.
And the family-- the parents didn't know, neither his parents nor her parents.
So then when they were married, my mother did-- she said she was very scared.
[LAUGHS] And when they were married, he sent a telegram to his parents that he just got married.
And my grandmother, surprisingly, did not put her head in the oven-- [LAUGHS] when she found out.
And they went to the train station to meet them.
And then she-- she hugged my mother and she says, well, I was against it, but it's done now.
There's nothing I can do about it, so I accept you as my daughter.
And--
Yes, she converted.
And she was actually very good.
She had a-- she learned for quite a with a rabbi.
And she was very good in Hebrew.
She did-- I remember her helping me with the prayers when I was little.
She knew everything.
She kept a kosher house.
She was wearing a sheitel-- had her head covered.
Very strict, for many, many years.
Very strictly kosher.
And she knew everything about Judaism.
And it pleased her.
She liked it.
She always said she was a Lutheran, originally.
And she told me once that the Lutheran religion never really appealed to her that much.
She always had her doubts about it.
But she says, when she was exposed to Judaism, she felt, here's a religion that I can really go for, and it made a lot more sense to her.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she felt very good about it.
Oh yes, very much so.
My father even-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father even had students.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He taught on Shabbat afternoon.
They came to the house and he was their teacher.
He would do Talmud.
He would study Talmud with them and thing.
We would keep Shabbat very strictly, and all the Jewish holidays.
I remember.

My great grandfather who was supposed to be-- my father's grandfather-- was a very-- a very religious man, and he was-- and I-- we still, even though we lost most of our belongings, I still have some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he had-- he had a huge Jewish library and some objects.
Old menorah.
We have an old menorah that still uses oil and wicks instead of candles.
That's how old it is.
And-- but we-- we observed all the holidays very strictly.
That I can very well remember.
My father took care of his parents who were elderly.
They lived with-- with them.
They had their own private rooms, and it was a very large apartment.
I can still see the apartment.
We had a view over the Alba, which was a river in Hamburg.
A nice view.
And we had my brother, who was one year older.
So I was about-- I had just turned five when my parents left, so I was little, of course.
And my parents, and we had-- a live-in-- one live-in maid, and we had-- I had a nanny.
I remember her.
She actually came to visit us once after the war.
She took me-- she took us for walks and I remember going to the park with her and going to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother still worked part-time at my father's office.
You know, she would-- she would not work full-time after she was married, but she still-- she enjoyed it, so she went occasionally.
She was not Jewish.
I don't know what religion, or if she had a religion.
I don't know.
I remember visiting her mother once.
She took me to her mother, who was an elderly woman.
And I remember her taking me to the office-- my father's office.
And I remember her taking me to the port, you know, looking at the ships, going to park.
I can still see, very clearly, parts of Hamburg-- walks and things we used to do.
No, they did not.
There were-- there-- Jews were-- the Jews we knew, we were in contact with, and my family-- my father had a large family.
They were all mostly killed.
But they were mingled with non-Jews, and they were assimilated in everyday life.
But we kept-- they kept our religion private.
So it was two different things.
Just like many-- most American Jews are American and Jews.
So we were Jews on Shabbat.
We were Jews on the Jewish holidays.
And we would eat kosher, and we would practice the Jewish traditions.
But for the rest, we were not dressed different, and we would not act different, and we were just part of the community.
Community life.
And as a young girl, you went-- you said you left Hamburg when you were five.
Did you attend school before you left in Hamburg?
No.
No, because I had just turned five.
We left a month after my birthday.
My birthday is in April.
We left in May.
Oh yes, public school.
Children would go to public school, not to, necessarily-- well, they had private Jewish schools too, parochial schools.
But it was not-- the more-- it was more common to go to a public school as a Jew.
And part of the-- just like in Belgium later, it's the same thing.
I went to a public school too, and there were many Jewish children in the school.
Yes, we had-- of course, we had lots of toys.
And I still have some-- I still have some children-- some German children's books, very old ones that go back a long time.
And children, of course, you know, we were also raised in the German tradition-- discipline, listening to the parents, doing the things right, and watching your manners.
I still remember some of the things my nanny or my parents would-- would teach us, or my mother, you know.
Do this.
Don't do this.
Hold your-- don't-- don't make a fist when you hold your spoon.
Sit upright at the table.
Don't talk, only when you're being I asked.
You know, we were much more disciplined than American children.
[LAUGHS] That's a fact.
They believed in discipline, not necessarily in physical punishment, but discipline.
And that goes for schools, too.
Not until it happened, yes.
I remember two events-- one event that was-- that really told me that something was about to happen.
To begin with, I can remember looking out of the window at night.
And number one, I noticed that adults would become very worried.
And they would talk a lot, and they would discuss, and they were getting very serious about it.
So I noticed that there was something-- something going on.
I remember at night, the-- the first Nazi troops, the SA marching with their-- with their lights.
What do you-- with their torches, the torch marches, and singing the antisemitic songs.
And we would watch-- look out of the window and it was scary.
Even as a little child, I noticed there was something scary going on, something threatening.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember, also, one event.
I was in my grandmother's room, and my grandmother had a friend over, her age, an elderly woman.
And they were whispering.
And I was supposed to take a nap, and I acted like I was sleeping, but I was not sleeping.
I was listening to them.
And I remember the woman looking at me at one point and saying, that poor child.
So I know-- I said, why a poor child?
I knew something was about to happen.
Something-- something bad was about to happen.
So that must been shortly before we left.
What year was that?
And another event that taught me-- that taught me that my life was going to be changed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We must have been in Belgium already, after-- when we arrived and we stayed in a hotel the first days.
And until then, I was used to people catering to me, everybody being friendly, and everybody trying to do nice things to me, and I felt like I was something special.
And I was playing in the hall with my brother, and we were probably making noise, you know, chasing each other or whatever.
And a maid came over and told us very severely you can't do that here.
You can't play here.
And I thought, a maid telling me, you know.
I know-- I knew, I felt-- I felt, instinctively, that my life was at a turning point.
You know, this was the end of the spoiled life and it was going to be serious from there on.
I felt that.
As a little child, I got the message.
That, I don't remember too much about, not in detail.
I just-- like I said, I noticed that everybody was very nervous and very-- and looked very unhappy, and that something dramatic was about to-- was happening.
But I did not know exactly what that was, and-- and-- and I don't remember it in detail.
I only know-- see little-- little pictures, and little scenes here and there.
I remember a train.
I remember a train station, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] but not in a-- in a continuing way.
All I know is that he was entitled to take very little money.
The bank accounts were blocked.
We took most of the furniture and-- and belongings, but money-wise, as far as I know-- I don't know the details-- but I don't think my parents were in very good shape.
And when my father arrived in Belgium not knowing the language, not being able-- we were officially accepted as political refugees, so they were very generous.
We were the first official political refugees to be accepted in Belgium.
That's why we got all privileges.
And we were even offered help, you know, from Jewish groups and committees.
But my-- my father was too proud to accept it.
That was not his style.
He wanted to do it on his own.
And of course, it was very difficult for him to know how to make a living.
He had two children.
He could not practice law.
And even if he had been able to, he didn't know the language that well.
He-- he has struggled for many, many years, and my parents always kept the appearances.
They rented a house that was a big, beautiful house in a nice neighborhood, with all our nice furniture.
And nobody would have guessed, you know, that we were not done-- that we were not doing well financially.
Nobody had cars in those days anyways.
I would wear clothes, you know, hand-me-downs mostly, but they were nice condition.
Everything was-- was looking well.
We always looked very nice.
And I know that my father had-- went through very, very hard times.
Eventually, he got a job as a legal adviser in a company that was owned by another former German Jewish refugee, who came to Belgium and made it very big.
He had a plant, a chemical-- he was a chemist and he had a chemical plant, and he-- he did very well.
And he hired my father as a legal adviser.
I always wondered if he really needed a legal adviser or he did it to, you know, help him.
It's possible.
Until the war, my father was there.
He had his own office there, and he had a nice salary.
And so then, he finally, you know, was able to relax, financially.
Antwerp.
Yes.
I started school at six.
My father checked out the best public school in the city.
You could pick any school you want.
You did not have to go in your district or in your neighborhood.
So he picked the school that was quite a distance, but it had a very good reputation.
They had schools for boys and girls separate in those days.
So it was a girls' school, and the school had two sections.
Belgium has two official languages, Flemish, which is a little like German, and French.
And my father wanted me to go through a French-speaking section, because he figured French is more important.
But it was not possible, because they have a law that says you have to be taught in the language you speak at home.
And when they found out that we spoke German at home, they said, she has to go to a Flemish section because it's closer to German.
Can't go to a French section.
My brother, however, being a boy, was considered more important as far as his education, so he was sent to a private school to learn French, to have his education in French.
And I went to-- to a Flemish school.
And at first, I remember being this absolutely terrified the first day of school because it's scary to begin with.
Number two, I couldn't understand a word that was being spoken.
I couldn't understand the speak-- the teacher.
I couldn't understand the other kids.
So I felt completely isolated from everybody else.
And there, they didn't cater to you.
You learned another-- you spoke another language, you had to adjust.
They did not-- you know, they did not have bilingual teachers or anything.
But the result was that, within a few months, I was fluent.
Not only that, but I was the first in my class.
And I remember my parents, how proud they were.
The first, we had two-- two tests, two exams one year.
And the first exam was before Christmas, in-- in winter, and the second one was before the summer vacation.
And the first exam, I was the best in my class.
We had 33 students in the class, and I was the best.
And I was already fluent.
And the teacher came up to my parents.
I remember-- I still see her and telling them how proud she was of me and how well I did.
And at first, I remember, when we learned the alphabet, for instance, and they had these little pictures to make the words.
And I could not-- I didn't even know the word, what it means, because I didn't know the language.
So I always tried to-- to sneak, you know, at another student to see what they were doing, to-- because I didn't understand it.
But because I had to, I-- I-- I did it.
You know, I had no choice.
I had to work harder.
Were there other Jewish children going to your school?
Yes, quite a few.
Quite a few.
And as a matter of fact, in winter, we had this rule that I didn't have to come to school.
I was excused on Saturday.
We-- we had to go to school on Saturday, half a day.
Being Jewish.
But you had to prove that you were religious.
If you went not religious and you didn't care and you didn't keep the holidays, you had to go.
Only if you kept the holidays.
So I was excused on Saturday.
Of course, I had to catch up and-- and-- and check out what they were-- what they did, and then, you know, catch up with the-- with the class.
And on Friday night in winter, we were allowed to leave earlier.
And everybody else would envy us.
So they would say, the Jewish-- Jewish children, get ready to leave.
So we left about an hour before the rest of the class.
School was till 4:00, and later, 5:00, and starting at 8:00 in the morning.
We had long school days, and we worked hard.
It's a lot, a lot stricter than here.
I remember-- I don't know if we were regular.
Yes, we attended.
The synagogue was called-- was named after the street.
I remember we belong to Ostenstradt-- that'd be East Street-- Ostenstradt Synagogue.
But I remember on Shabbat, my father taking me to services, once in awhile, to Hasidic services.
And since I was little, I was harmless.
I was allowed.
They didn't-- they didn't object in the-- in that-- for me to go in the male section.
And I watched them, and it was very interesting.
My father was always a little interested in, also, in the Hasidic way of praying, even thought he was never a Hasid himself.
So he went to different types of-- of synagogues.
So I don't know if we really belonged to any one in particular, because he liked to explore.
And I remember watching.
I always loved to watch people, watching them whatever they were doing and praying, and-- and their mannerism while they were praying and all that.
I thought it was very interesting.
And then he would tell me, never mind.
And I had a hard time following the prayers, and he says never mind.
Just say the Shma and say the Shemona Esrei, you know [LAUGHS].
That's it.
And then we would walk home later.
And--
Flemish.
German.
Yes.
Flemish.
At home, with my parents, I always spoke German.
We kept speaking German.
And in school, I spoke Flemish.
And we were not allowed to, but in Antwerp they also speak-- speak a dialect, a Flemish dialect, which people speak among themselves.
And later, gradually, I also learned to speak that.
But in school, we were-- if we were caught speaking it, we got in trouble.
They would not let us do it.
So [LAUGHS] we had to speak decent language.
French.
He learned Flemish, but he always spoke it with a-- some kind of an accent.
And I was like, you know, a native.
I spoke it eventually like a native.
And later, I learned French in France.
My parents and my brother.
Oh, and a cousin who had been staying with us.
My father's niece-- one of my father's nieces.
And she was with us at the time visiting for several months or whatever.
And my parents took her along and she stayed with Belgium several years until her parents also managed to leave Germany and went to England.
And as a matter of fact, I still have a cousin in London from that family.
Her sister [INAUDIBLE], she and her sister actually both still live in London.
She stayed with us for several years.
And she took care of my brother and me almost like a nanny, you know, and stayed with us.
Because she didn't-- my parents thought it was safer.
She stayed with us rather than staying in Germany with her parents.
Well, it was pretty nice.
It was pretty nice.
I had friends.
I had good friends from school and I had friends in the neighborhood.
We played.
I never experienced antisemitism, not that I recall.
And I had friends-- I had Jewish friends.
I had non-Jewish friends.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I felt quite comfortable.
And gradually, I completely assimilated.
And to me, it became like my native country.
I felt very comfortable.
Between five and 12, when the war-- when the war started.
So that was the end of my normal life.
Yes, we still corresponded with relatives in Germany.
And I remember my parents-- listening to my parents talking, and they tried to urge them to come, to get out.
My father, for instance, had two cousins-- first cousins.
One was a judge in Hamburg.
One was a lawyer.
One was a banker.
The banker managed to leave on time, Alarion, and went to Israel.
And he actually had a bank there until he died, Alarion Bank, in several cities in Israel.
He managed to survive.
Another cousin who was a judge, and was-- the lawyer died of-- he died of a natural death, which was lucky for him.
And the judge got deported.
And at one point-- and they never made-- they didn't survive.
At one point, my parents wanted to adopt a child.
I think it was their child, their daughter.
She was about my age.
She was supposed to come to Belgium and stay with us.
And then, they-- they got caught.
They all got caught and deported, and never made it.
And I remember being happy about it because I always wanted a sister.
And to me, it would've been wonderful.
But they never made it.
They couldn't get out.
My grandparents, luckily-- my grandfather had died already before we left, of natural causes.
He was in his 80s.
My grandmother, who was about 70 or in her early 70s, refused to leave.
She stayed behind in the apartment, and she said, I'm an old woman.
They're never going to harm me.
Little did she know.
I have my graves here.
My husband is buried here.
I want to stay.
And she came to visit us once in Antwerp.
And I remember, I was impressed because she was from Holland, originally.
She was Dutch.
And she spoke Flemish.
And I didn't realize my grandmother could speak Flemish.
And I was very, very proud of her.
And about a year later, she died.
I remember having the shiva.
And my father later said, thank God she died of a natural death, just before, you know, they would have deported her probably.
In '38-- I think up to '38-- Jews were still able to leave.
And after '38, there was like a-- the trap was closed.
They could not leave anymore after the Kristallnacht.
No, no.
It was much too dangerous.
Much too dangerous.
We never set foot.
My mother once went back to her father's funeral, and that's a horror story in itself, in 1938.
And she hardly made it back.
Yeah.
